blog_index.title: IBD Blog | Crohn's, Colitis, Nutrition & Daily Life | IBDPal
blog_index.description: Free patient-education articles on Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis: diet, flares, teens, school, treatment basics, and wellness. Not medical advice.
blog_index.intro: Practical articles for people living with inflammatory bowel disease. Every post is educational only. Work with your gastroenterologist or IBD team for personal care decisions.

hubs.title: IBD Nutrition & Diet Hub | Crohn's & Colitis | IBDPal
hubs.description: Nutrition and diet resources for Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis: flare foods, FODMAP, hydration, protein, and patient guides.
hubs.intro: No single diet fits every person with IBD. These guides and articles help you explore patterns many patients discuss with their care team.

sections.heading: Start here
paragraphs: If you are newly diagnosed, pair nutrition reading with our newly diagnosed hub and a food-symptom log.
See how IBDPal sets baseline nutrition targets in our DRI article and curated research (AGA, NIH DRI, Congress) on the Research tab or at /research.

guides.label: What should I eat with Crohn's or colitis?

hubs.title: Crohn's Disease Hub | Symptoms, Diet & Support | IBDPal
hubs.description: Crohn's disease patient education: diet during flares, treatment basics, support groups, and free tracking tools from IBDPal.
hubs.intro: Crohn's disease can affect any part of the digestive tract. Use these guides and articles to prepare for clinic visits and daily life questions.

sections.heading: New to Crohn's?
paragraphs: Visit our newly diagnosed hub and printable visit prep checklist before your next GI appointment.

guides.label: What to do during a flare

hubs.title: Ulcerative Colitis Hub | Diet, Flares & Support | IBDPal
hubs.description: Ulcerative colitis education: UC diet, flare management, triggers, support near you, and symptom tracking with IBDPal.
hubs.intro: Ulcerative colitis primarily involves the colon. These resources focus on nutrition, flares, and finding peer support.

sections.heading: Track what matters
paragraphs: Logging stool patterns, urgency, and foods may help you and your team spot personal triggers.

hubs.title: Teens & High School with IBD | 504 Plans & Social Life | IBDPal
hubs.description: Help for teens with Crohn's or colitis: high school 504 plans, nutrition, social life, exercise, and pediatric resources.
hubs.intro: IBD during high school means planning for bathrooms, absences, growth, and friendships. These articles are written for teens and parents.

sections.heading: School planning
paragraphs: A Section 504 plan can document bathroom access, medication storage, and flexible attendance. Meet the school nurse early each year.

hubs.title: IBD Flare Help | What to Do During a Crohn's or Colitis Flare | IBDPal
hubs.description: Calm education for IBD flares: first 48 hours, gentle foods, hydration, sleep, and when to call your clinic. Not emergency medical advice.
hubs.intro: Flares are frightening but manageable with a plan. These guides and articles cover early steps many patients discuss with their IBD team.

sections.heading: When to seek urgent care
paragraphs: Heavy bleeding, severe pain, fever, signs of dehydration, or rigid abdomen need prompt medical evaluation. Use your clinic's on-call instructions.

faq.title: IBD FAQ | Crohn's & Colitis Questions | IBDPal
faq.description: Answers to common Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis questions: diet, flares, Crohn's vs UC, support, and tracking. Education only.
faq.intro: Short answers to searches patients often run. Always confirm personal decisions with your gastroenterologist or IBD care team.

items.q: What is the difference between Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis?
items.a: Both are inflammatory bowel diseases (IBD). Crohn's disease can affect any part of the digestive tract from mouth to anus. Ulcerative colitis primarily involves the colon and rectum. Symptoms overlap, but treatment and surgery options may differ. Your GI team names your diagnosis and activity level.

items.q: Is there a special diet for Crohn's disease or ulcerative colitis?
items.a: There is no one-size-fits-all IBD diet. Many people adjust fiber, fat, dairy, or specific triggers during flares versus remission. An IBD-focused dietitian can help you reintroduce foods safely. IBDPal supports food and symptom logging to bring patterns to appointments.

items.q: What should I eat during an IBD flare?
items.a: Many patients temporarily choose gentler, lower-fiber foods such as white rice, bananas, broths, eggs, and lean protein when symptoms are active. Hydration matters. Diet does not replace medication. See our flare help hub and talk with your clinician.

items.q: Is IBD the same as irritable bowel syndrome (IBS)?
items.a: No. IBD (Crohn's and colitis) involves visible inflammation and requires medical treatment. IBS is a functional disorder without the same inflammatory damage. Some people have symptoms of both. Only your care team can clarify your diagnosis.

items.q: Can you play sports or exercise with Crohn's or colitis?
items.a: Many people in remission or mild disease stay active with pacing and hydration. During flares, rest and gentle movement may be better. Coordinate school sports with 504 documentation if needed. See our exercise article for general education.

items.q: What is a 504 plan for IBD in school?
items.a: A Section 504 plan can document accommodations such as bathroom access, extra time, medication storage, and flexible attendance for students with Crohn's disease or ulcerative colitis. Work with the school nurse and your clinician's letter. Not legal advice.

items.q: How do I find Crohn's or colitis support near me?
items.a: Start with the Crohn's & Colitis Foundation chapter finder and support group search. IBDPal lists state-level support pages and a community map. National helpline: 888-694-8872.

Biologics are advanced medications that target specific parts of the immune system to reduce inflammation in Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis. They are prescribed and monitored by your GI team. Insurance prior authorization is common.

items.q: Should I track food and symptoms with an app?
items.a: Many patients find logging helps them notice patterns before clinic visits. IBDPal is a free iOS app for food, symptoms, and visit prep. Tracking supports conversations with your team; it does not diagnose or treat disease.

items.q: When should I call my doctor during a flare?
items.a: Call per your clinic's instructions for worsening pain, significant blood, fever, dehydration signs, or symptoms that feel different from your usual flare. For emergencies, use local emergency services.

support_index.title: IBD Support by State | Crohn's & Colitis Resources | IBDPal
support_index.description: Find Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis support by U.S. state: CCF chapters, helplines, pediatric networks, and IBDPal tools.
support_index.intro: National resources help every state. Many areas also have Crohn's & Colitis Foundation chapters and local support groups. Educational directory only; verify details with each organization.
